title: Bazlama
subtitle: Turkish yeasted flatbread cooked on the hob
excerpt: Bazlama is a thick, soft Turkish flatbread, yeasted and slightly tangy, cooked in a dry pan on the hob. It is thicker and chewier than lavash, closer in texture to a thick pitta but without the pocket. In Turkey it is sold warm from griddles at market stalls; at home it comes together in well under two hours and is best eaten within a few hours of baking.
sourceNotes: Bazlama is a staple of rural and street food in Turkey, particularly in central Anatolia. References to thick griddle breads appear in Ottoman kitchen records; the bread is described in Claudia Roden, 'The New Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1985) and in Nevin Halici, 'Turkish Cookbook' (1989). The yoghurt in the dough provides the slight tang and helps the bread stay soft.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooled. Reheat in a dry pan or briefly in a warm oven.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly; work through more batches on the hob.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dough can rest in the fridge for up to 24 hours.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: cooked in a dry frying pan on the hob over medium heat

content.text: The yoghurt replaces some of the water and gives bazlama its characteristic slight sourness and soft texture. Plain natural yoghurt works; full-fat gives a slightly richer crumb. The dough is softer than most bread doughs; it should be slightly tacky but not so sticky that it clings to the surface. Add flour by the teaspoon if needed.

Combine 400 g plain flour, 4 g yeast, 6 g salt, and 5 g sugar. Add 150 g yoghurt and 120 ml tepid water. Mix to a soft dough, then knead for 6 to 8 minutes until smooth. The dough will be softer than a standard bread dough. Cover and leave at room temperature for 60 minutes until doubled.
Knock back and divide into 6 equal pieces, each about 110 g. Shape each into a ball and rest for 5 minutes. On a lightly floured surface, roll each into a disc about 20 cm across and 3 to 4 mm thick.

Heat a large dry frying pan over medium heat. Cook each bazlama for 2.5 to 3 minutes per side until charred spots appear and the bread is puffed and cooked through. The bread may puff up in places; press it flat with a folded cloth if it becomes uneven. Stack in a clean tea towel. Best eaten warm, within a few hours.

items.solution: The gluten needs a rest. After dividing into balls, leave for 5 minutes before rolling. If tearing continues, leave a further 5 minutes.
The pan is too hot. Medium heat is the target. Cast-iron pans retain heat; reduce the flame slightly between batches.

items.problem: Bazlama is stiff and dry after cooling
items.solution: Stack in a tea towel as soon as they come off the pan. The steam trapped inside keeps the bread soft. Wrap in a cloth immediately; do not leave on a cooling rack.
